summary: DHH addresses the common fear that remote work leads to organizational drift because nobody knows what anyone is working on. He argues the solution is far simpler than more video calls: just ask people automatically and regularly. At Basecamp, they use automated check-ins with two questions—what did you work on today, and what will you work on this week—answered by the whole company. This creates transparency far beyond what traditional stand-up meetings achieve, serves as an early warning system for struggling employees, and enables serendipitous cross-team collaboration.
key_points: Two simple automated questions—daily and weekly—replace the need for status meetings and management-by-walking-around
Making progress reports company-wide rather than boss-to-employee fundamentally changes organizational transparency
Automated check-ins serve as an early warning system when someone is stuck, since weak or vanishing answers signal trouble without requiring someone to explicitly ask for help
Cross-team pollination happens naturally when everyone can see what others are working on, replicating the 'water cooler' effect remotely
money_quotes: The way to know what people are working on is simply to... ask them! Automatically and all the time.
If there's one thing we've learned during this pandemic, it's that even people who like meetings don't like virtual meetings in the same quantity.
While a traditional stand-up meeting might communicate status to a handful of people, these automated questions can easily do so to fifty people or more. That's a fundamental change.
When someone is stuck, it's often hard to explicitly ask for help, but when their answers make clear that progress isn't happening, it serves as an implicit way of saying the same.
It's easy to get so caught up in the daily tasks that you forget to zoom out and gauge whether you're actually making progress on that big picture you're intend to paint.
Asking people what they've worked on and what they intend to work on frequently and automatically gives the company a daily pulse of progress.
key_insight: Remote work transparency doesn't require more meetings—it requires simple, automated, company-wide check-in questions that replace synchronous status updates with asynchronous visibility.
